sentences.text: Now we came over the borders.
The borders were open.
The trains were free.
And we could go search for family.
So naturally, we went back to Lodz.
And we went back, back to Lodz, to the apartment that we had left only nine months ago.
There was a Polish woman in the apartment.
Part of the ghetto was destroyed.
But most of it was standing as it was.
And Polish people just took over.
They just moved in and took over the apartment and every-- all the belongings there.
We came-- Karola went to her place to look for her family, and the other girls went to their homes.
And I came into the [POLISH] 18.
That's where we-- that was the last address.
And there was a Polish woman.
She put her foot in the door.
I told her who I was and that I used to live here.
And she says, it's all mine now.
I didn't want anything.
I just wanted to know if anybody looked for me, if any pictures, any-- any-- I could see the way she was standing there that the furniture-- in fact, that furniture was my Uncle Baruch, Baruch.
Before he left for Russia, he had a chance to take his furniture.
Well, he lived in the area that was outside of the ghetto, before we came to ghetto.
He had beautiful furniture.
So he managed to get that furniture before the Germans took over his apartment.
So it was in our house.
It was supposed to be for safekeeping.
That what my mother was holding on to this-- safekeeping.
And I could see the furniture.
Everything was in the same spot we left it.
And then I had asked her what happened to the books.
And she said, we tossed it in the garbage.
And she showed me outside where-- where the garbage was.
And she said, your people came and took the books.
And those people came and had found pictures of my uncle and my brother Laibele.
And I went to the cemetery [SIGHS] to Laibele's grave, because I knew where the grave was.
And I found a metal headboard of a child's bed.
[BACKROUND NOISE] I put this as a tombstone.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We had to leave.
I slept one night.
Karola came back.
She couldn't find anybody.
The other girls couldn't find anybody.
So we slept in a high school, on the floor.
And as we were sleeping, they asked-- somebody had said that the people that was taken out from the ghetto May 1942 and September-- during this specific time-- were called the shpera.
During the shpera were sent to Chelmno.
And those were experimental gas chambers to put them in trucks with a red cross on it.
They thought they were going for medical-- for physical examinations before they go to work.
And [SIGHS] they put gas into those trucks.
So I found out how my mother died.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We couldn't remain in Poland.
I went to a place called Wroclaw-- the German it was Breslau.
Because the transport from Russia was supposed to come there.
They allowed the Polish Jews to leave Russia.
So I went there hoping to find my sisters and my brother.
And I met Morris-- Moishe.
And we were married within six weeks.
We couldn't hold out to wait for my sisters and brother, if they were alive.
You didn't even know if they're alive.
Because there was this-- the Polish anti-Semitism-- anti-Semitism never-- never ended.
And there was the [POLISH], AK for short, that was killing-- they were fighting the Germans.
They were fighting the Russians.
But now they were killing the Jews again.
And they were attacking Jews wherever they could find them.
And we had to smuggle our way out.
Coming in, the borders were open.
We could come.
We could go.
But out, we couldn't go.
We had to go between a truck of furniture, between-- a Russian truck driver put us between furniture and smuggled us over to Berlin, to the Russian side.
And from there we went to the American side, to the displaced persons camps.
He took everything he could.
First of all, we had paid him whatever money we had.
There were a few other people with us.
And we all gave whatever money we could.
And while we were on a certain spot, the border check whatever, he left us standing there.
And he came out, and he said, give me your [NON-ENGLISH]-- watches.
And whoever had a watch, he would take the watch from them.
Because we had-- didn't have any money anymore.
It-- it seems like the watches, in a way, saved us.
Because he went in during another spot-- border check.
And he was getting drunk there.
And we were worried that he's going to betray us.
And he's-- and we don't know what he's going to do to us.
And then he came back.
And he said he bribed them with the watches.
So I guess the watches, in a way, came-- came in-- made use of him.
Because this was already a-- we were liberated in May.
And when we are being smuggled, it was already 1946, April.
So this was almost close to a year.
So he's-- you know, they started finding jobs to do some business.
And we kind of, you know, got watches and clothing, and-- and tried to-- tried to build-- to rebuild.
No, no.
[SIGHS]
To Berlin, Schlachtensee.
In Berlin, Schlachtensee, Berlin, that was-- there had the displaced persons camp.
And we would come-- we came to that camp.
As we came off in the train, the-- the truck driver gave us some money to pay for the train.
So there's always a little good in-- in everybody.
That's me, right, the optimist.
[LAUGHS]
And we paid for the train.
The Germans looked at us-- again, what are you doing?
You're dirtying up our city.
They didn't say it, but you could see it in their faces.
And when we came off the train in the American side, we didn't know where we were going.
And there was somebody, he came over and said, [YIDDISH]?
Jews?
And he was waiting for transports.
He took us to the Schlachtensee, to-- to the displaced persons camp.
So he was there always welcome for those that made it out of Poland, to the American side.
What were your experiences in Berlin, in the-- in the DP camp?
[SIGHS] We lived in long-- [SIGHS] I don't know if it was barracks, a building-- I know, I remember there was long buildings.
We were on the second floor.
And we were put in, Morris and I, into an apartment with a woman that had a child.
And just to see a child-- there were very, very few children.
That was a-- a-- just a miracle.
And that-- the story when I stared at the child, and then later the mother told me that this child is a miracle.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The mother and father were sent to Auschwitz.
Before they sent to Auschwitz, they had a chance to smuggle their child out of the Warsaw ghetto.
The child, I think, was two years old.
If I'm not-- if I remember correctly, her name is [?
Marelka. ?] I had written down her name.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The parents were at Auschwitz.
They didn't know who survived, who didn't survive.
The child was with a Polish family.
And then the lady-- they gave it to another family-- they gave it to the church to protect it.
And somebody betrayed him and said that the church is hiding Jewish children.
So they took the child, and they gave it to a peasant family, not telling them that this is a Jewish child.
They said, oh, it's a Polish orphan.
And when, by miracle, by miracle of miracle, the father and the mother both survived.
And they found each other.
And they went to look for the child-- start tracing back their steps.
And they brought him to the place where the child was raised by the Polish families.
Several years had passed.
That child-- when she saw the mother, she said, what happened to your beautiful hair?
Because this mother always had long hair.
So she remembered this, that the-- the-- that the mother had long hair.
She recognized her.
But that Polish woman put up a fight.
She didn't want them to take the child.
And well, they did take the child.
But that child was so full of hate against Jews.
They told her the Jews are the devils.
And we had a hard time showing her that we are not devils, because-- and she kept on crossing herself and doing the prayers.
And the mother would turn away the head [? and say ?] what can she do?
Eventually, she kind of warmed up.
And but it-- but this really was a miracle child.
And just to see, it was this Jewish child that survived, and it was an anti-Semite.
They made it into a little anti-Semite.
And it-- it took a while.
And hopefully I think that she did come back there, where she belonged.
We got a message that my brother-- my husband's brother, that somebody saw him in Wroclaw, which-- Breslau, Wroclaw [INAUDIBLE] two names, which was the wrong message.
Because somebody saw him that he was alive and he was going to Israel.
And we-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Morris wanted to go look, naturally, for his brother.

And We asked them to send us to-- to Munchen, because they knew that people-- according to the information, he was-- in fact, they said that my friend Karola and her husband Oscar-- that they saw him in Wroclaw.
Because [INAUDIBLE] in Wroclaw and we left.
So we managed-- the camp sent us to Munchen.
And it turned out later, when we found Karola and Oscar, they never saw him.
They never heard of him.
But meanwhile, we were shipped to a different camp in Munchen, which was a transit camp.
Because in-- in Munchen we slept in barracks, almost like in Auschwitz.
It was a military camp, and-- Did you get to feel better?
Did you have enough to eat in the camps?
Yeah, yeah, food, yeah.
Food-- they had a lot of foods that we were used to-- canned foods and richer foods that we were used to.
But no, food wasn't a problem anymore.
You know, and did we feel better physically?
Yes.
We were getting stronger, yeah.
Not in those two camps.
He had a job later on.
But from that camp, where-- because Munchen was a transit camp.
So they send us to Pocking, which was also-- every place was military barracks.
They were all former either concentration camps or military camps.
And over there, too, we shared-- it was a long barrack.
And we shared with another couple.
We slept on a bunk bed, the two of us.
On one bunk bed, the other couple.
On the other bunk bed-- and we were both pregnant, the two women, with sleeping in-- on bunk beds.
At that time, he didn't have any work yet-- didn't get work till we were-- in this-- in this camp, my son Laibele was born, August 27, 1946.
Wonderful.
We were so anxious to have a new Jewish generation.
Because we always wondered, will we be able to have children?
And just the idea there's a new Jewish generation, and we are here to have children, it was wonderful.
It was such a joyful occasion.
Everybody-- every time a child was born, everybody celebrated.
A midwife.
[SIGHS] And she was very patient and-- and very caring.
In fact, during delivery, I received a letter that mine sister is alive.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [SOBBING] with-- the letter was from Chana.
But she was telling us about Mala and Yankl.
And as soon as we found out that-- where they are-- and they, too, smuggled their way out of Poland and came to the DP camp.
So eventually, we all got together.
In fact, [SIGHS] Laibele was born in Pocking.
But my sister Mala came to Berlin and went to the same journey.
But she went to Leipheim.
And she came with a carriage.
Laibele's-- one of the pictures, we have the carriage.
[LAUGHS] But we wanted to be together, so we all arranged-- it wasn't that simple.
Because everything was red tape.
We arranged to be moved from Leipheim.
Chanele came with her husband, [?
Morris. ?] And we were all assigned to go to-- to Leipheim.
And in Leipheim, we all moved into one room with Mala and Yosef.
And they had a little boy, Avrom, which was seven years old at that time already-- which I first met him.
And I had Laibele, and Chana and Moniek.
So we had six grownups and two children, all in one room.
[SIGHS] Undescribable.
And-- and way, you know, other people, kind of where we could see people looking at us with envy.
You can't blame them.
You can't blame them.
Because very few found anybody.
So if they envied us, they had good reasons.
And we just stuck together.
We cooked together.
We did everything together.
[SIGHS] Several years, to-- from '46 or beginning of '47 to '49.
I think to about April '49.
That's when Morris got a job.
He got a job as a policeman for the DP camps, or the-- our own police, you know, and just as the things he did-- things were-- weren't in order.
It wasn't police as-- police as we know it in here, just, you know, security guards, or something like that.
I got a job as a kindergarten teacher to the few Jewish children that were there, because just the children that were just like Abe-- or [?
Abramach, ?] we called him.
He was born in Russia.
There were a few children that were born in Russia or they were in hiding, [? so the-- ?] of kindergarten age, four or five years old.
The rest are just children that were born in '46 or-- or '47.
And-- and I had pleasure in teaching them to sing Yiddish songs, and to clap hands, to see them smile.
So that was my job.
In the ghetto I-- I wasn't a kindergarten teacher in the ghetto.
And this-- I don't know what the title over there was.
My former kindergarten teacher, Mrs.
Musta, ?] had in the ghetto formed a kindergarten-- hold on-- held on to the children.
And she had ask me, as one of her children, which was now, you know, older, to come and work with her, would help her there.
So I was kind of experienced-- put it this way-- to work with-- with children.
I don't think you needed much experience.
You just needed love.
Because all those children needed is love, to feel secure.
He was born in [?
Leffeld, ?] because Leipheim was being liquidated.
So that's why we always moved from place to place, because they were liquidating.
And he was born July 7 in [?
Leffeld. ?] And at that time, we were hoping to be able to go to America.
So between red tapes and medical problems, he was born there, rather than being born in the United States.
But we were waiting to go to the United States.
Well, actually, we waited five years [SIGHS] to find-- first, we had to find a sponsor.
[SIGHS] We found a sponsor through a sheer miracles, because we did-- had no addresses.
We-- we didn't have anything to prove our relations or who we are.
And Morris remembered that he had an uncle-- an aunt, his mother's sister-- that lived somewhere-- somewhere outside of Boston.
That's as close as came.
I read the in the DP camp, the Jewish daily newspaper.
And a person that worked for the was a friend of my parents.
He managed to escape in 1940, before we were put into the ghetto.
And I sent a letter to him telling him who I was-- he knew who I was-- to put an ad in the saying that Moishe Senderowicz-- this is our original name-- is looking for the family Borenstein.
He put it in the And the also was published in Boston.
And somebody read the there.
And he called up that uncle-- the aunt had already passed away.
We didn't know that-- do you know a Senderowicz?
He said, yeah.
And that's how we found our sponsor.
In fact, his daughter [?
Lily-- ?] we just saw her this weekend.
She was-- came to a wedding of a grandson.
We went to see her at-- at the hotel.
But through sheer miracles, we got-- what, it took five years.
And even with the sponsor, you still need-- needed to HIAS to help financially and a lot of red tape.
And meanwhile, they started testing me.
And they said I might have tuberculosis.
Everything kind of dragged, and dragged, and dragged and-- and everybody is-- was looking where to go.
Wherever-- whatever country is gonna open the doors, that's where they want to go.
So we came-- we left first, [SIGHS] the first from the four families.
And then-- no, Yankl left first.
He was signed up as a tailor to Canada.
So he left first.
Later, he sent papers for Chanele, because he could sponsor them.
I left to America first.
And then [?
Molly ?] came to the [INAUDIBLE].
And then eventually, they came from Canada to the United States and we found each other.
But for seven years, we didn't know who is alive, who is dead, if we're ever gonna find each other.
And then when we are separated, we didn't know if this is permanent or someday we'll get together again and we'll find each other in the United States, so--
February 2nd, 1950.
[SIGHS] Laibele was three.
And Avrom was seven months old.
Well, I had two more children.
There are four children.
I have three sons and a daughter.
Harvey and Nancy-- they were both born in the United States.
We moved from Boston, because we were sent to Boston.
We moved to-- from Boston to New York, because I wanted to be with my sisters.
Even when we lived in Boston, I went to night school.
Morris went to school.
I came home and studied from his papers.
Because first, we had to learn English.
We came to a family [? that ?] that spoke Yiddish.
But they were more comfortable with English.
So we started to kind of quickly-- and it-- it's a good way of learning a language.
You have no [LAUGHS] other way.
And so we picked up English easily.
And I started doing homework on my-- on my own, and then went to-- to night school.
And when we moved to New York, because I wanted to be with my sister [?
Molly, ?] again started taking English for foreign-born-- more courses.
And then later, I went to Jewish Teachers' Seminary.
I always wanted to be a teacher and from a Jewish school system, maybe because I grew up in a Jewish school system.
I always wanted to be a writer.
And I accomplished this, too.
So some people-- their dreams the hard way did come true.
I am teaching, I'm lecturing, and I'm writing.
[SIGHS] I wrote three books.
I'm working on the fourth one about my experiences.
I'm lecturing a lot because the books are being read all over the world.
But I lecture in the United States.
I was invited to Canada.
I was invited to Switzerland.
But it seemed to me like it was too long a journey, too complicated.
But they're using the books there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I lecture a lot because when the kids read the books, and when I come in-- after I kind of-- and I come without notes.
So if I could speak today, the next day I'll speak of something else, whatever I feel important, that pops into my mind.
But my favorite part is when I give them time for questions.
I always allow time for questions.
Because it's a challenge.
I don't know what they're gonna ask.
Some questions are painful.
Some are wonderful.
Some are challenging.
And-- but I like that part.
I have thousands and thousands of letters, artwork, gifts, phone calls just to hear my voice.
And it-- it is so touching, like if somebody-- and they're surprised.
When I answer the phone, they expect us-- I don't know who I am that I must have a million secretaries.
She-- two little girls called up.
And one says, you know, she wanted to speak to Riva.
[LAUGHS] I know who they're talking about.
I says, this is Riva.
And she starts, it's her, it's her.
I guess there was another little girl.
[LAUGHS] And we're just standing there, didn't know what-- and so, OK, OK, calm down.
I have to calm them down.
And it-- that's so such a wonderful feeling.
And I get phone calls from all over.
They're doing reports.
They want to interview me.
One little girl is-- I think it's from Missouri.
She called two weeks ago.
She had called several weeks ago that she's participating in some kind of a history-- women in history, some history month, and they-- groups.
And it-- they're going for certain levels.
So she called a few weeks ago.
We up to national level.
Can I have your voice on tape?
I'm putting a recorder and speak to me, interview.
And she was so thrilled.
And then she wrote me that they're going to Wash-- no, to Maryland.
Could I come to see?
They're going to the finals.
And I couldn't come.
But it was so wonderful that, you know-- that you touch lives.
I have a lot of beautiful things that have send me to show.
And I'm very touched with the artwork, what they express with their letters.
So--
I [SIGHS] am the principal of a small Sunday school, where we teach Yiddish, Jewish culture.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I take special joy out of knowing-- and quite often, I express it when we get together with the parents.
For holidays we get together-- that this-- not only this generation.
The parents weren't supposed to be born.
There weren't supposed to be any Jewish generations.
And here, the child that wasn't supposed to be is here, survived.
And I teach generations.
And some of the generations are already marrying-- you know, the older children-- that I-- I-- it just gives me the greatest pleasure to-- to see those generations, and sometimes also the sad side.
When I look at these children, I could see cattle cars.
I could see them go to cattle cars.
And I think I work harder to speak and to-- and to teach.
Yeah.
A quarter of a million paperback.
And I don't have the statistics of the hardcovers.
Between the three hardcovers must be about 20,000.
And just the paperback is a-- a quarter of a million.
And this is books that were being-- that were sold.
Imagine how many people read it that just borrow from one another, or schools where they're just being passed on without re-- you know, buying new-- new copies.
It's just-- it's just unbelievable.
A cousin that didn't know existed, because though we were in the ghetto together.
And we were children.
And he was even younger.
He was 10 years old.
I didn't know him, because the families, for whatever reason-- I don't know.
We-- actually, we third generation cousins.
The grandfathers were brothers.
So sometimes those relations kind of fall apart.
And we found, again, everything is by sheer accident.
They went to Miami.
There's the Holocaust Memorial in Miami Beach.
And there are names written down.
And we put the names of those who perished.
And they saw the name Minsky.
For a while, he didn't even know that he was a Minsky, that his mother's name was a Minsky.
But he went back to Poland.
He found out when he was born.
He didn't even know where-- when he was born.
He knew he was born in-- in-- he thought that he was born in Lodz.
He knew he'd lived in Lodz.
But for some reason, he spoke German.
He-- he didn't know why he spoke German.
So in the camp, they assumed he was a German child, and they gave him a birthday-- January 1.
They figured January is the first month, first day is easy to remember.
They looked at him-- ah, you look about that-- you're born 1930.
That was his birthday.
And then finally, later, he found out, when he came back to Poland, years later from the United States, that he was born in Lodz, and who his parents were, and that his mother's name was Minsky.
And when he saw Minsky on that wall, he wanted to trace who are those Minskys.
And it turned out that we are related.
And he has a wonderful story that I think it should be-- I think all the stories should be told.
But I'm talk-- I'm writing about his story, in connection with mine-- how we found each other.
And-- and sometimes, we really don't have to say anything when we talk to each other, in fact.
And his wife was born in Russia and smuggled her way out of Russia.
And then she was in Israel.
And he says, when I tell to her, she sometimes says things-- she doesn't understand it.
When I say it to you, you know what I mean, what I'm saying.
Because she says, so why didn't you do this?
Why didn't you do this?
There is something between survivors that sometimes you don't have to-- you say one word and they know what you're saying and what not to ask, or what to ask.
You know, so--
[SIGHS] Because those who deny the Holocaust-- and I had phone calls from so-called historian telling me that I'm a liar.
My books are a lie.
There was no Holocaust.
There were no gas chambers.
And I get a lot of literature-- in fact, I have literature in my little library there, which are from Holocaust deniers.
I received letters from a student-- I received 50 letters a couple weeks ago from southern New Jersey.
